video_title: How AI Agents Are Transforming This $140 Billion Industry
gist: Joe Lonsdale interviews Outset founder Aaron Cannon and 8VC partner Jack Moskovich on AI-moderated customer research: software that runs one-on-one AI interviews at survey scale but with interview depth. Outset grew 8x revenue last year, serves 100+ enterprises (Microsoft, Nestle, Google, Uber), and argues AI is the 'killer application' for the market-research industry.

insights.claim: Market research underpins essentially every product and package a consumer encounters, making it a large but tooling-starved market that Outset argues is ripe for AI disruption.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Frames research/insights as a broad, underpenetrated software category rather than a niche, supporting a large TAM thesis for AI-native research tools.
insights.quote: research like underlies everything that we interact with in the world

insights.claim: Billions of surveys are run each year and are, per Outset's founder, the lowest-quality customer data available, defining the incumbent product AI-moderated interviews aim to displace.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Positions the survey incumbents (e.g. NPS/survey vendors) as vulnerable to a higher-fidelity AI substitute.
insights.quote: there are billions of surveys done a year and they are the lowest quality data you could imagine

insights.claim: The enabling 'why now' for AI research is two simultaneous capabilities: computers can now conduct spoken conversations and can process large amounts of unstructured information, removing the human-time bottleneck on both interviewing and analysis.
insights.speaker: Jack Moskovich
insights.so_what: A clean two-factor test investors can apply to judge which AI applications became newly viable versus hype.
insights.quote: computers can now talk to people and computers can also process large amounts of unstructured information

insights.claim: Outset's core value proposition is resolving the research industry's central trade-off, delivering the scale of a survey with the fidelity of a manual one-on-one interview via AI-led interviews.
insights.speaker: Jack Moskovich
insights.so_what: The 'solving a fundamental industry trade-off' pattern is the investment thesis the VC uses to identify category-defining companies.
insights.quote: you can have the scale of the survey with the fidelity of the manual interview

insights.claim: Traditional qualitative research was extremely labor-intensive: 90-minute interviews, a handful per week, projects spanning two to three months, with each transcript read four times, meaning enterprises made multi-billion-dollar decisions off tiny, manually-processed samples.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Quantifies the manual cost structure Outset compresses, illustrating the productivity delta driving enterprise adoption.
insights.quote: every transcript I read four times

insights.claim: AI-moderated interviews show much lower participant fatigue than surveys because the AI listens and plays back responses, so people stay engaged for an hour without dropping off.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Higher completion and depth is the data-quality moat over survey vendors and a metric to watch in the category.
insights.quote: people will go for an hour and they won't drop off

insights.claim: Outset built its first prototype on OpenAI's Da Vinci (the earliest GPT model) which barely worked, and the product now spans video, screen sharing, and multimodality as models have improved.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Product capability is directly leveraged to frontier-model progress, a tailwind but also a dependency on foundation-model vendors.
insights.quote: we built our prototype on I think it was on Da Vinci

insights.claim: People disclose more to an AI interviewer than to a human because AI removes social-desirability bias, the same effect that skewed political polling when respondents hid unpopular voting intentions.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: A structural data-quality advantage that is hard for human-led research to replicate, differentiating Outset's output.
insights.quote: people actually share things with AI that they won't share with the person

insights.claim: Outset's first production study was for Weight Watchers' growth efforts, chosen partly because respondents are more candid with AI about a sensitive weight-loss journey, surfacing emotional drivers usable in marketing.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Concrete proof point showing where the honesty edge translates into commercial marketing value.
insights.quote: our first customer ever, our first study ever like in in in pro in production was with Weight Watchers

insights.claim: The same candor advantage extends to expert-network-style calls, where AI moderation could get closer to ground truth than paid human experts who often overstate their access or authority.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Suggests a potential expansion market into expert-network/diligence workflows used by investors.
insights.quote: there is an advantage of getting closer to ground truth at all times

themes.theme: Enterprise GTM and the tipping market

insights.claim: Selling to large enterprises means selling a new behavior rather than replacing an existing tool, so Outset goes to market consultatively and uses 'forward-deployed researchers' embedded with clients.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Signals a services-heavy, high-touch GTM motion investors should factor into gross-margin and scaling expectations.
insights.quote: We actually have an idea of forward deployed researchers at our company

insights.claim: Enterprise AI sales must reconcile three speeds simultaneously: fast-moving technology, moderately-moving humans, and slow-moving enterprises.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model for pacing AI enterprise go-to-market and setting adoption-curve expectations.
insights.quote: you're dealing with all three speeds at the same time

insights.claim: The market for AI-moderated research tipped in the last 12-18 months from default skepticism requiring education to a demand-unconstrained state where large research teams already want it, which Outset calls the 'killer application' for AI in insights.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: A category-timing signal: the education phase is over and adoption is now pull-driven, favoring the incumbent category creator.
insights.quote: this is the the killer application for insights and research with AI period

insights.claim: Outset has over 100 major companies including Microsoft, Nestle, Google, and Uber using it to interview tens of thousands of people, and has out-competed AI labs offering multi-million-dollar comp packages for talent.
insights.speaker: Joe Lonsdale
insights.so_what: Blue-chip logo concentration validates enterprise product-market fit and pricing power.
insights.quote: Over 100 major companies are using them to interview tens of thousands of people

insights.claim: AI research is not an automation/cost-reduction play like customer support because research demand is not static: better research drives revenue, so companies reinvest, making it a growth driver rather than a cost center.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Reframes the unit economics: expanding rather than shrinking budgets, implying larger long-term account value.
insights.quote: It's a growth driver, not a cost center

insights.claim: The researcher role bifurcates into a barbell, with people either building research infrastructure ('laying the pipes') or acting as expert methodologists interpreting data, rather than doing manual execution.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Counters the 'AI eliminates researchers' narrative, a labor-market read relevant to adoption resistance.
insights.quote: we kind of see like the barbell where they're either like playing a big role in infrastructure

insights.claim: Outset envisions replacing static NPS programs with always-on AI listening that continuously interviews customers, detects when something breaks and why, and eventually triggers fixes automatically.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Points at disrupting the entire NPS/CX feedback stack, a large adjacent budget.
insights.quote: it is like what what it should be is a constant on constant listening to customers

insights.claim: Outset foresees a 'self-driving research' loop where a falling NPS score triggers AI interviews, synthesis, and hand-off to a coding agent like Cognition to fix the underlying product issue, with a human signing off.
insights.speaker: Jack Moskovich
insights.so_what: Illustrates agent-to-agent workflows (research agent feeding coding agent) as a near-term direction for enterprise automation.
insights.quote: It's this whole kind of idea that we're building is is self-driving research

insights.claim: Outset treats synthetic research and 'digital twins' as a complement, not a replacement, for human interviews: twins are built from real AI-moderated interviews and periodically validated against the real person, avoiding pure public-data modeling.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Stakes out a defensible position in the hyped synthetic-research debate: proprietary interview data feeds the twins, not scraped public data.
insights.quote: the more you do the digital twin work, the more it tells you we don't know stuff

insights.claim: Outset grew revenue 8x last year on an exponential trajectory and nearly doubled headcount this year from about 40 to roughly 75 employees, with more than 25 engineers.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Hard growth metrics anchoring the company as one of 8VC's highest-growth AI portfolio companies.
insights.quote: the trajectory continues to to be exponential

insights.claim: Outset treats culture and people as the last durable moat, with both founders personally meeting every hire, resulting in an over-80% offer acceptance rate and zero regrettable churn since founding, even beating AI labs' multi-million-dollar packages.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Talent-retention metrics are a leading indicator of execution capacity in a hot AI labor market.
insights.quote: we have a more than 80% acceptance rate of our offers

insights.claim: Outset's founders are 'allergic to overhead,' avoiding rigid engineering pods in favor of swarming on two-week priorities, which the investor cites as the first team he has seen accelerate rather than slow down as it hires rapidly.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cannon
insights.so_what: Operating model that decouples velocity from headcount, a rare scaling signal investors prize.
insights.quote: Michael and I are absolutely allergic to overhead
